did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an automated framework involving Multi-Head Self Attention Layer-based Vision Transformers (ViT) for feature extraction and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for classification to classify skin lesions into nine classes (including melanoma, BCC, SCC, etc.) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and presents a novel automated framework using Multi-Head Self Attention Layer-based Vision Transformers and an MLP for skin lesion classification. It reports original experimental results, including training accuracy (98%) and testing accuracy (93.22%), on the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES datasets, along with statistical analysis and comparison with other studies.

title: An automated framework to classify skin lesions using Multi-Head Self Attention Layer-based Vision Transformers
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated framework for classifying skin lesions into nine diagnostic classes, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, using Multi-Head Self Attention Layer-based Vision Transformers (ViT) for feature extraction and a lightweight multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for classification. The methodology incorporates contrast stretching for image enhancement and Region of Interest segmentation to improve input quality. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES datasets, achieving a training accuracy of 98% and a testing accuracy of 93.22%. Performance was further validated through hold-out and 5-fold cross-validation, as well as comparison with existing state-of-the-art methods. The results demonstrate that the proposed ViT-MLP architecture provides robust and scalable performance for multimodal skin lesion detection.

all_task_types.target: Nine diagnostic classes of skin lesions (SCC, BCC, NV, AKIEC, MEL, SEK, DF, BKL, VASC)
all_task_types.details: Multinomial classification of skin lesion images into nine distinct diagnostic categories using a Multi-Head Self Attention Layer-based Vision Transformer for feature extraction and a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for classification. The study reports class-wise precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy on the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES datasets, along with multiclass ROC curves and AUC values.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (SKIN-HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, medically curated by the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna. It contains images in jpeg format.

use.training: Combined with PAD-UFES; classes with fewer than 1,000 images were up-sampled to 1,000 to create a balanced training set of 18,000 images (9 classes x 2,000 images each, though the text states 1,000 per class resulting in 9,000 combined before augmentation, then up-sampled to 1,000 per class for a total of 9,000 images for training process, later stating 18,000 images for training process after augmentation).
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance.
use.other: Source of 7 of the 9 diagnostic classes.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20 (PAD-UFES)
datasets_used.description: A skin lesion dataset composed of patient data and clinical images collected from smartphones, containing data from about 1,373 patients with 1,641 lesions. It comprises a total of 2,298 images across six diagnostic classes.

use.training: Combined with HAM10000; classes with fewer than 1,000 images were up-sampled to 1,000 to create a balanced training set.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance.
use.other: Source of 2 additional classes (SCC, SEK) not present in HAM10000.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the model's performance on the combined HAM10k + PAD-UFES, PAD-UFES, and HAM10k datasets.
To evaluate the model's performance on the combined HAM10k + PAD-UFES, PAD-UFES, and HAM10k datasets.
To assess the statistical significance of the proposed model's performance against random chance (baseline) across per-class accuracies for the combined, PAD-UFES, and HAM10k datasets.
To generate multiclass Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under the Curve (AUC) values for multinomial classification.
To evaluate the model's performance and class-wise accuracy on the PAD-UFES and HAM10k datasets.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve and Area Under the Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To signify the model's ability to distinguish between different classes and evaluate performance for the combined, PAD-UFES, and HAM10k datasets.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: The model's classification performance is attributable to random variation (random chance).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The model's classification performance is statistically significant and not attributable to random variation.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Z-scores obtained (44.98 for combined dataset, 23.06 for PAD-UFES, and 11.03 for HAM10k) far exceeded the critical thresholds of 1.96 (95% confidence) and 2.58 (99% confidence), confirming that the model's performance is statistically significant well beyond conventional significance levels (p ≈ 0).

Statistical Significance (Z-score).notes: Z-scores exceed critical thresholds of 1.96 (95% confidence) and 2.58 (99% confidence).

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-Head Self Attention Layer-based Vision Transformer (ViT) combined with a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)

encoder_components: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU)
architecture_details.attention_maps: 12 attention maps corresponding to the number of Transformer layers

conclusions: The proposed automated framework using Multi-Head Self Attention Layer-based Vision Transformers (ViT) for feature extraction and a lightweight multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for classification achieved a training accuracy of 98% and a testing accuracy of 93.22% on the combined HAM10000 and PAD-UFES datasets.

The model demonstrated robust performance across nine distinct skin lesion diagnostic classes, including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma, with high class-wise precision, recall, and F1-scores (e.g., 99.47% accuracy for Melanoma and 100% for Vascular lesions in the combined dataset).

Statistical validation using Z-score hypothesis testing confirmed that the model's performance was statistically significant (p ≈ 0) and not attributable to random chance across the combined, PAD-UFES, and HAM10000 datasets.
The study highlights the scalability of the ViT-MLP architecture, suggesting its potential effectiveness for broader arrays of diagnostic classes in future research.
The integration of contrast stretching enhancement and Region of Interest (ROI) segmentation facilitated efficient feature extraction and improved input quality for the classification task.
important_remarks: The study combines the HAM10000 and PAD-UFES datasets to create a nine-class classification task, but PAD-UFES images were captured using smartphones, introducing potential domain bias and variability in image quality compared to the medically curated HAM10000 dataset.

Class imbalance was addressed by up-sampling classes with fewer than 1,000 images to 1,000 using augmentation techniques (zoom, flips, brightness, distortion) after the train-test split, which may lead to data leakage or overfitting if not strictly managed, although the authors state the split was performed before up-sampling.

The model achieved high accuracy on the combined dataset (93.22%) but showed significant performance disparity across classes; for instance, on the HAM10000 dataset, Benign Keratosis-like lesions (BKL) had a low recall of 46% and accuracy of 45.55%, indicating poor generalization for this specific class.

The PAD-UFES dataset contributed only two classes (SCC and SEK) not present in HAM10000, while other classes were combined from both sources, potentially masking dataset-specific biases or distribution shifts.
The paper reports statistical significance using Z-scores (p ≈ 0) but does not provide confidence intervals for the accuracy metrics, limiting the assessment of result precision.

The proposed framework is described as lightweight and scalable, suggesting potential for mobile deployment, but no computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time) or hardware-specific deployment tests are reported.

The study focuses on multinomial classification of nine classes, which is clinically more complex than binary melanoma detection; however, the clinical relevance of detecting less critical lesions (e.g., DF, BKL) with high accuracy is not discussed in terms of diagnostic utility.
